text: Instruments were set up outside a 4-ft-thick lead-glass window.
Although the window was not specially selected, the readings were within $1.0\%$ of the true dimension.
Use of a calibration chart of the window variations reduced the error to $\pm 0.1\%$ .

The principal advantages of this system are: (1) parts may be measured without direct contact or contamination, (2) convenient and comfortable working areas can be utilized, (3) the cell interior is entirely free for useful working space, and (4) acceptable accuracy is attainable.
The purpose of this test was to determine the feasibility of taking accurate measurements with an optical tooling system through a 4-ft-thick lead-glass window.
Optical tooling instruments were set up in front of a new 4-ft-thick hot cell window.
An alignment scope generates a reference line to which the 20-power transit scope is set perpendicularly at the vertical plane and elevation of interest.
The transit scope and alignment scope are set respectively $90^{\circ}$ from and parallel to the window (Fig.
2) along the steel optical flat, and repeating the perpendicular setting of the transit scope to this mirror.
It has been estimated that the combination tooling bar and all the associated equipment for one complete setup would cost about $6000.

section: Typical Example of Measuring a Part
text: A cylinder was placed in the cell at a point about 5 ft back from the cell window, and the diameter was recorded using the optical tooling instruments.

section: Typical Example of Measuring a Part
text: Table 1 shows a first and a second try, with the cylinder moved to a new position for

section: Typical Example of Measuring a Part
text: The cylinder was $6.800 \pm 0.002$ in, in diameter and had a shiny surface, which was not considered the ideal for best accuracy, but was used as an example of practical system evaluation.

During the calibration of the cell window, the complete cycle of raising the tooling bar, precise leveling, squaring with the optical wall mirror, taking 18 sets of readings on the horizontal scale using the transit square, and also taking 18 corresponding points vertically with the optical micrometer attachment were made in about 1 hr.
8, show a dispersion of about 0.030 in. for length measurements of 20 to 30 in., which indicates a reproducibility of about $\pm 0.1\%$ .
